question: Does the swarm's epistemic architecture match any established epistemological framework?
method: Map 5 knowledge tools against 4 epistemological traditions, identify gaps
prediction: At least 1 tradition provides vocabulary for gaps swarm hasn't discovered
result: CONFIRMED — 16 gaps found across 4 traditions, 9 actionable

reliabilism.note: Most vulnerable tradition. f_gam2_reputation_integrity.py attempted but abandoned (L-601 voluntary decay)

social_epistemology.note: Increasingly urgent as multi-agent features expand (steerers, F-MERGE1, concurrent sessions)

high_severity_gaps: Process reliability tracking (reliabilism) — no feedback loop on tool/method accuracy
Testimony trust model (social epistemology) — human and steerer inputs unweighted by reliability
Peer disagreement protocol (social epistemology) — no formal framework for concurrent session disagreement
Degree of corroboration (Popperian) — weak vs severe tests treated equally
verdict: Prediction confirmed. Swarm is heavily Bayesian, partially Popperian, weak on reliabilism, gap-exposed in social epistemology. Reliabilism is the practical bottleneck — knowing which processes produce accurate beliefs would catch more errors than any other gap.
